segments.text: You agree?
If she wishes, just take away question from us.
Yeah, fair enough. Very good.
Okay, I'll, um, I'll take questions away from Lauren.
Well, they, they're on the same,
Same footing.
So Jeffrey, welcome you.
Thank you very much for coming
and, um, helping us to piece through, um,
our own thinking as a committee on this.
Uh, I'll hand over to you,
but as you can see, um, a number of committee members have,
uh, questions for you.
So you ask time for that.
Well, um, I, I, you were all young
long ago when I was in this parliament, these
questions of regulation were
begg in those days.
We had the economic stabilization Act of 1948
that allowed everything that moved to be regulated, uh,
without resort to parliament at all.
Uh, and that was done as,
as Sir Robert Muldoon once famously told the times
of London, if you hang your hat on economic
stabilization, you can do anything.
And he did. We had
all wages regulated at one stage, all interest rates
and even carless days.
Now, you are all too young to remember that,
but I can tell you that is executive government, the like,
of which we've never seen before.
That led me in the second edition of Unbridled Power
that was published in 19
and 97 to
cause those laws that we had.
We, we had made lot of use of delegated legislation
for many years.
The distinction that was made between
before primary legislation
and delegated legislation was secondary legislation was
vital to this whole discussion.
And I was very pleased
to hear the legislation design committee.
I, I chaired the pre it's pre desist of that committee.
Um, and, um, when I was at the Law Commission, now,
let me say that the Economic
Stabilization Act, the Commerce Amendment Act of 1979
and various other, the National Development Act
that allowed you to develop New Zealand by order and council
and not by parliament.
These were very grave exercises
of executive power.
And that led to the repeal
of all those statutes.
And it also led to the setting up on this committee,
which was part of the Labor Government's policy
for the 1984 election.
And the committee got to work
and was pretty active for a long time.
And it had bipartisan support.
And when this, I won't characterize it
in unfair terms,
but with the regulatory standards bill
appeared in this parliament, it was exceedingly difficult
to understand what it was going to try and achieve
because it seems, uh, if you look at the 75 page
regulatory impact statement, you find even
that the officials of the minister's own department
didn't agree with the bill.
And indeed, one of the reasons why they might not have,
there was nothing said about the Regulations Review
committee in, in the legislation,
or indeed, as far as I can see in any of the consideration
that led to the drafting of this Ill considered Bill.
Now, the, the truth of the matter is that
the disallowance of regulations, which is what the aim
of this legislation, the regulatory standards bill purpose,
is to reduce the amount of legislation, not directly,
but indirectly, by producing a lot of reports
that don't have legal effect,
but which are given to people who
do have decision making capacity.
Uh, and, and of course that is, uh,
a bit like the Chinese water torture.
You will have a lot of reports coming
and you'll get nervous about it if you're a minister.
And indeed, it, the, the, the effect of the, of the bill,
uh, is to, it's, it's actually designed
to split ministerial responsibility.
Ministers are responsible for the secondary legislation
that they administer from the primary act.
And I think the suggestion that the, this leg,
that the regulatory standards will be restricted
to primary legislation's, entirely sound,
otherwise, you're gonna have a confused miss.
The, the conduct
of this parliament already pretty unsatisfactory in many
points of view, uh, is going to get a whole lot worse
when you have this, these confusing areas of responsibility
that don't fit.
And I thought the previous speaker was extremely tactile the
way he put that, but I'm telling you
that confusion is the worst thing that you want.
And and I, I have to say
that the power to disallow is
not exercised very often.
And one of the things that worries me about that is
it leads to people saying that regulations are too frequent.
Because when they are made, they're not disallowed
as the Parliament has the power to do
and has had for many years.
Uh, that was another part
of the Labor Party policy in my day
to set up the Regulations Disallowance Act,
and it's not used much.
And why isn't it? I'll tell you the reason.
There's a lot of jurisprudence with the regulation,
with the Regulations Review Committee.
It's even published at the university.
It's been over a period of years, developed quite well.
The bad habits of New Zealand legislation have been somewhat
restricted, uh, by the activities
of this committee, but not enough.
Now, the difficulty with the,
with the new bill is that it promises far too much
and it cannot deliver it
because it's a vast amount of, let me just go
through the amount of law that we now have on the books.
It's astonishing.
We have, um, uh, amount of law, which, uh,
which the Parliamentary Council has, uh, has
said is it's
pages of primary legislation.
That's millions.
It's 25 million
that comes from Parliamentary Council now,
and most of it is, uh, public act.

provincial council stuffs still on the books.
If you're gonna get rid of legislation, you have
to have a policy of doing it.
And it's very time consuming,
and that's why it's not done enough.
And to, to remove unnecessary statutes from the book,
you need to give a job to the Law commission
and get them to research whether they're still needed,
what the legal effects would be if they were
not, and get rid of them.
But that has never been done
because New Zealand, which loves passing law,
even when it's not necessary, uh, wants
to pass more law all the time,
but never get rid of the law that it has.
And that leads in an old democracy like this

and they are unmanageable.
Mm-hmm. And, and if this, if this legislation
of from the regulatory standards bill goes,
what I don't know how the legislation advisory committee is,
is, um, I, I don't know
how the regulatory standards bill is ever going to
coordinate with you.
It's impossible. Mm-hmm. Now, that leads me to this.
If the aim is to
curb primary legislation, that is one thing.
If the aim is to curb secondary legislation,
that is another thing.
And I think the secondary legislation is the one
that will create the most confusion
because of its new, the number of regulations
that are made it, I haven't given,
given the figures for that.
They're enormous. You cannot run
a country on the basis of primary legislation alone.
It is not possible.
And the ministers have to be able to have the ability
to have administrative arrangements
that are within the competence of the
enabling provisions in the primary act that allows detail
to be dealt with, because you can't do everything
by primary legislation.
So I just think
that the disallowance provisions are highly relevant
to the whole enterprise of the regulatory, uh,
standards bill, and, and they need to be made part of it.
And, and if you, if you are left alone, that would be good.
But what you need to do is to be more muscular.
If, if we are going to have economists complaining
without any evidence being profit,
that there is too much regulation,
then you are the people to stop it.
The reason it isn't stopped is that ministers don't like
that because it means that they're not able to, to,
uh, do what they want to do.
And ministers like to be able to do what they want to do
because they think that's why they're there.
And that's true. So, so you see, I,
I just can't see, I can't make any sense of the mess

It is absolutely irresponsible if, if, if,
if there is not some policy changes made to try
and actually deal with the problem,
not the Ima imagined problem.
The Parliament has the power right now in the legislation

four, to disallow a whole range
of statutory regulations.
Now, you know better than I,
you don't recommend it very often.
I saw you did it the other day for a legal, a legal, um,
education partly deserved it, um, about legal education.
The more Parliament keeps out of legal educ the better.

there is just a lot of work to revise

It's not to create a great big mess

Yeah. And,
and so I, I just, uh, wanted to give you that background

important long ago, it somehow hasn't stuck.

might make them feel better about the Parliament.

Yes, absolutely. Point, because

Abide by. Uh,
but it also has an Attorney general needs provide a

Back to Parliament. But Parliament can ignore that

Yes. Uh, so with this bill, um,
that we now considering this

I'm interested in your thoughts about, uh, how Juris may

Yeah. So we're having a, I don't know,
what's the 25th anniversary?
The Got Rights Act in Canterbury in 1st of October.
And it was, they asked me to tell, tell 'em what I thought.
I think it should have been entrenched,
and I still think that Mm.
And, um, if you're going to have human rights,

And, and the idea that you're going to be able to, uh,
somehow slough off it
because of the, uh, what the features of the world rights

I think in the small democracy with the executive,

and that cannot ever be fixed

The theory of Westminster government is that the

And we stopped doing that.

that's got about 23 members in it.
Um, and, and,
and so it's, it's just, it's just
people just want to be ministers.
They don't want to be back benches here.

Uh, and, and this is a very small country, and,

Just, just on on that though, the, the, um,
New Zealand Bill of Rights Acts has

That's a defining aspect of being an economist.
So I just think that you really do need to look at

The Law Society gave it a real,

and thank you so much, uh, CVI Palmer

secondary legislation should just not be included.

But if it were to retain the way it is right now

Who has the final say?
Is it RRC

This committee is a creature of parliament

And, and, and that, that is a very important thing

Because if you don't have a purpose,
you will cease to exist.
The committee was much bigger.
I seem to recall when we started, it's,
it's quite small now,

'cause it probably doesn't have glamor, uh,

'cause like you said, it's Committee of Parliament,

Uh, and, and the legitimacy, uh, comes from being

and you are the guardian of ensuring that the law,
making power is not abused by the executive,
and the executive can abuse it.

And, and, and, and that is
because ministers

Yeah. And often the,

That's the problem. Mm.

And and that's really at the essence of this whole debate.
And, and it's, it's, it's a debate

And I thought that's why the officials didn't agree

even though their ministry had only been recently set up.
They didn't agree with him. And he's got another line of it.
And, and I, I don't, can't see

They, you need to have harmony with both these things.
The point should have been made since I've been here

You have a responsibility.

Be pardon? We,

Yes. Mm-hmm. Um,

P Walters, thank you

To be, um, to be filed along

Yes. Um, which I think the clerk spoke to,

No, I agree with That. Um, and so if that's the case

it isn't going to work.
It's a piece of, um, it's a piece of
we don't like the courts

Mm-hmm.
Um, so Jeffrey, we are at time,

Is now, um, we are, we are struggling to find consensus.

Well, I think if you took the l d's first point

secondary legislation, that's the beginning

because the statutes are the ones

Uh, and, and when it gets down to secondary legislation,

Thank you for being so generous. Well, I

Wednesday 16 July 2025

Regulations Review Committee

Briefing on regulatory reform
Hearing of Evidence
Sir Geoffrey Palmer
